3A_Bibliographic.citation: Stockdale, L. A., Burr, K. L., & Doxey, R. A. (2024). Pet ownership, loneliness, and screen media use in the ABCD study: Contemporaneous and longitudinal associations. Anthrozoos, 37(6), 1053-1072.

3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: Data from 11,875 participants from the ABCD study, release 5.1 were examined... The ABCD study recruited 11,875 children ages 9-10 years at baseline from 21 sites across the US.
3B_Sample_Data.notes: Sample size varies across analyses due to missing data

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Screen media use (total weekday screen time, total weekend screen time)
3C_Exposure.modalities: TV/movies, videos, video games, texting, social media, video chatting
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: Screen Media Use. Youth reported their typical screen media use on a weekday and a weekend day on the ABCD Screen Time Questionnaire. Total weekday and weekend screen media use variables were created by summing time spent on each of the six activities.
3C_Exposure.notes: Screen time is treated as both exposure and outcome in different models; pet ownership is also an exposure

3D_Outcome.primary_outcome: Loneliness and screen media use (bidirectional; pet ownership associations)
3D_Outcome.outcome_instrument: NIH Toolbox Loneliness scale (child-report and parent-report)
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: Loneliness was measured using the NIH Toolbox Loneliness survey, which included both child-report and parent-report versions. The child-report version included five items... and the parent-report version included five items.
3D_Outcome.notes: Multiple outcomes: loneliness and screen media use are each treated as outcomes in different models

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Cross-lagged panel models using linear mixed effects with random intercepts for site and family
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: age, sex, race/ethnicity, household income, caregiver education, site (random), family (random)
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: Cross-lagged panel models were used to examine the longitudinal associations between pet ownership, loneliness, and screen media use across three waves. Site and family were included as random effects to account for the nested data structure. Models controlled for age, sex, race/ethnicity, household income, and caregiver education.

3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Weekday screen media use at baseline positively predicted child-reported loneliness at year 1 (beta = 0.02, p < 0.05)
Weekend screen media use at baseline positively predicted child-reported loneliness at year 1 (beta = 0.03, p < 0.05)
Child-reported loneliness at baseline positively predicted weekday screen media use at year 1 (beta = 0.02, p < 0.05)

3G_Reporting_Quality.abstract_significance_verbatim: Significant cross-lagged paths emerged between screen media use and loneliness
3G_Reporting_Quality.large_n_verbatim: The large sample size provides power to detect small effects
3G_Reporting_Quality.small_es_verbatim: While the effect sizes were small, these findings are consistent with the broader literature and suggest meaningful longitudinal patterns
3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: Standardized betas are very small (0.02-0.03). CIs not reported for cross-lagged paths. Paper primarily focuses on pet ownership as a protective factor.

3H_Recommendations.recommendation_verbatim: Pet ownership may be a protective factor against loneliness in youth and could be considered as part of family-level interventions to reduce screen media dependence.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion focuses primarily on pet ownership as a protective factor against loneliness and excessive screen media use. Screen time-loneliness associations are noted as bidirectional but very small. The paper frames findings within a social displacement and companionship framework, emphasizing that pets may buffer against loneliness and reduce screen dependence.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.ambiguity_notes: Effect sizes were difficult to extract precisely as many were reported only in figures. The paper's primary focus is pet ownership rather than screen time per se.
